text:
Pioneering sustainable aviation fuel producer LanzaJet was presented with the Renewable Fuels Association’s 2026 Industry Award on Feb. 26 at the National Ethanol Conference in Orlando. In November, the company became the world’s first to produce jet fuel from ethanol at a commercial-scale facility.

“Pioneering an entirely new technology means stepping into the unknown,” said RFA President and CEO Geoff Cooper. “It means years of research without guarantees, overcoming technical setbacks, earning trust from partners and regulators, and having the resilience to persist when the path forward isn’t yet clear. Innovation at this scale requires vision, courage, and an unwavering belief in what’s possible. This accomplishment is the result of 15 years of research, innovation, investment, and teamwork. By unlocking the potential of their proprietary Alcohol-to-Jet technology, LanzaJet has not only advanced the global deployment of sustainable aviation fuel but also created new opportunities for decarbonization, economic development, and energy security around the world.

“They have also shown that a tremendous new market opportunity for American ethanol producers and farmers is no longer just over the horizon. It is well within our reach. Their work exemplifies what’s possible when innovation, resilience, and purpose come together to solve one of the toughest challenges of our time.”

The LanzaJet Freedom Pines Fuels facility in Soperton, Georgia, has an annual production capacity of 10 million gallons of alcohol-to-jet SAF, and it remains the only biorefinery currently producing ethanol-based SAF in the United States. LanzaJet CEO Jimmy Samartzis was at the National Ethanol Conference to receive the award; Meg Witty, vice president of corporate and government affairs, represents the company on RFA’s board of directors.

title: Ghana poised to develop Sustainable Aviation Fuel industry …ICAO study reveals
description:
GHANA has significant potential to establish a Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) industry to help reduce carbon emissions in the aviation sector, a study by the International Civil Aviation Organisation (ICAO) has revealed.
SAF is an alternative fuel produced from non-petroleum feedstocks and is designed to reduce reliance on fossil fuels used in air transportation, thereby cutting greenhouse gas emissions.
The Director-General of the Ghana Civil Aviation Authority (GCAA), Reverend Stephen Wilfred Arthur, described the development as a significant milestone in Ghana’s journey towards a greener and more resilient aviation sector.
“Sustainable aviation fuel is not merely an innovation; it is a regulatory imperative.
As a contracting state, Ghana must align itself with global commitments while safeguarding the integrity of our aviation system,” he said.
text:
GHANA has significant potential to establish a Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) industry to help reduce carbon emissions in the aviation sector, a study by the International Civil Aviation Organisation (ICAO) has revealed.

The study indicated that the country’s abundant agricultural residues, existing refining infrastructure and strong regional trade linkages places it in a favourable position to develop a viable SAF sector capable of contributing to ICAO’s goal of achieving net-zero carbon emissions in international aviation by 2050.

SAF is an alternative fuel produced from non-petroleum feedstocks and is designed to reduce reliance on fossil fuels used in air transportation, thereby cutting greenhouse gas emissions.

Presenting findings of the seven-month feasibility study at a stakeholder workshop in Accra yesterday, an Independent Consultant with ICAO, Ms Damiana Serafini, said although Ghana possessed enormous feedstock potential, the materials were dispersed across the country, posing a significant challenge in attracting investors into the sector.

“Significant challenges, particularly around feedstock mobilisation, cost competitiveness, institutional coordination and long-term sustainability safeguards remain. However, the potential is here,” she stated.

She explained that the broader goal must be for the country to explore how it could transform its agricultural sector into an energy-generating sector, where residues from food production could serve as value-added inputs.

“It is not just about producing food, but also about taking advantage of agricultural residues as a value-added product for energy generation,” she stated.

Ms Serafini called for coordinated action among multiple stakeholders to realise the vision of developing SAF as a key milestone towards the 2050 net-zero target.

The Director-General of the Ghana Civil Aviation Authority (GCAA), Reverend Stephen Wilfred Arthur, described the development as a significant milestone in Ghana’s journey towards a greener and more resilient aviation sector.

He noted that as regulator of the industry, the GCAA had the responsibility to ensure that Ghana met the highest international standards in safety, security and environmental stewardship.

“Sustainable aviation fuel is not merely an innovation; it is a regulatory imperative. As a contracting state, Ghana must align itself with global commitments while safeguarding the integrity of our aviation system,” he said.

Rev. Arthur noted that the transition from feasibility to implementation would require the establishment of robust regulatory frameworks, including certification processes for SAF, quality assurance systems and integration into existing fuel supply chains.

This, he explained, would involve harmonising national standards with ICAO guidance, ensuring compliance with environmental policies and developing monitoring systems to guarantee transparency from feedstock production to fuel deployment.

The Director-General thus affirmed the GCAA’s commitment to collaborate with the Ministry of Food and Agriculture, the Ministry of Energy and Green Transition, as well as industry stakeholders, to craft policies that encourage investment while protecting public interest and safety.

title: A Path Forward for Conservation and Regenerative Agriculture - Bipartisan Policy Center
description:
The Agricultural Act of 2018 (2018 Farm Bill, Public Law 115-334) enshrined a focus on regenerative practices and considerations into federal agricultural policy for the first time in history.
Key principles guiding NRCS conservation programs should continue.
Takeaway Two: Key Principles Guiding NRCS Conservation Programs Should Continue.
Outcome-driven: Related to the principle above, agricultural conservation programs should primarily focus on outcomes, rather than some other metric (dollars invested, etc.).
Roundtable participants lamented that personnel shortages and inconsistent staffing levels have plagued NRCS conservation programs for decades, and those challenges persist today.
text:
Executive Summary

Policies to drive the adoption of agricultural practices that protect America’s land, air, and water emerged in the wake of the Dust Bowl of the 1930s with the creation of the Soil Conservation Service at the United States Department of Agriculture, the predecessor to today’s Natural Resources Conservation Service (NRCS). Over the past decade, Congress and USDA have expanded the scope of these conservation efforts and begun to focus on specific agricultural practices that can remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and reduce greenhouse gas emissions—including addressing methane emissions from livestock production, curtailing nitrous oxide emissions from fertilizer application, and storing more carbon in agricultural soils and trees.

These practices also can provide a host of environmental co-benefits, make agricultural production more resilient to extreme weather, drive much-needed investments into rural communities, and boost revenues for farmers facing tough economic conditions. As Congress moves toward an update of the 2018 Farm Bill in the coming months, these issues have once again been thrust to the foreground.

Policymakers, scientists, businesses, and non-governmental organizations view this suite of conservation practices—often grouped under the umbrella of “climate-smart” or “regenerative” agriculture—as a way to both mitigate emissions concerns and improve outcomes for both farmland and farmers. Congress and administrations led by both parties have begun to prioritize policies and investments to expand these regenerative practices. A continued focus on policy design, coupled with investment in resources and federal personnel to support this nascent movement, will be needed to ensure that the full promise of the economic and environmental gains from regenerative agriculture can be realized and sustained.

Introduction

Early in the 21st century, major players in the global agricultural community began to highlight that adopting certain agricultural practices may mitigate climate change compared to conventional production methods. (Figure 1 shows the potential scale of greenhouse gas emissions reductions and other environmental co-benefits from regenerative agriculture and other nature-based solutions.) In 2018, a seminal academic paper bolstered the case for increased focus and investment in nature-based climate solutions, including those in agricultural production systems. The Agricultural Act of 2018 (2018 Farm Bill, Public Law 115-334) enshrined a focus on regenerative practices and considerations into federal agricultural policy for the first time in history.

Figure 1. Climate mitigation potential of 21 Natural Climate Solutions in the United States

Source: Joseph E. Fargione et al., “Natural climate solutions for the United States,” Science Advances, Volume 4, Issue 11, November 14, 2018. Available at: https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/sciadv.aat1869

The Bipartisan Policy Center entered the rapidly evolving space of regenerative and climate-smart agriculture policy in 2021 with the launch of its Farm and Forest Carbon Solutions Initiative and Task Force. In the years after the task force convened, Congress built on the momentum of the 2018 Farm Bill, passing the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), which contained nearly $20 billion for USDA investments in regenerative practices, in 2022. Later that year, the Growing Climate Solutions Act, which provided a host of tools for farmers looking for new sources of revenue through private sector market opportunities that value climate mitigation and other environmental improvements, became law.

Building on these significant achievements, the task force went on to issue a series of policy recommendations to Congress in advance of the expected reauthorization of the Farm Bill in 2023. Portions of the Farm Bill were addressed in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA, Public Law 119-21), in 2025. However, the full Farm Bill’s update is now more than two years overdue, limiting the opportunity for Congress to make necessary updates to modernize conservation programs that underpin wider adoption of regenerative practices.

Against this backdrop of Farm Bill uncertainty, BPC convened a roundtable with former members of Congress, farmer and rancher representatives, food companies, NGO advocates, philanthropic leaders, and bipartisan congressional staffers in November 2025.

The group of approximately 30 participants began the discussion by outlining lessons learned over the past decade of efforts to drive regenerative agriculture. The roundtable’s ultimate focus and goal was to chart a path forward and highlight the conditions needed to accelerate and sustain adoption of these beneficial practices. Participants observed that November’s discussion was particularly timely in the wake of Congress passing another short-term extension of the 2018 Farm Bill, changing leadership priorities at USDA, and uncertainty regarding the status of the federal personnel required to administer and support these programs.

This summary highlights five key takeaways from the November 2025 roundtable:

Significant congressional and public support for these practices persists.

Key principles guiding NRCS conservation programs should continue.

Policy levers and markets outside of USDA can accelerate practice adoption.

Innovation should be prioritized, both for measurement/verification and new technologies.

Challenges related to personnel and efficient delivery of conservation-related technical and financial assistance remain.

Takeaway One: Significant Congressional and Public Support for These Practices Persists.

Roundtable participants from across the political spectrum uniformly agreed that broad support continues on both sides of the aisle for investments and policies to drive regenerative agricultural practices. Polling data indicates that the IRA’s provisions pertaining to climate-smart and regenerative agriculture investments were among the most popular with respondents across the entirety of the landmark legislative package.

While Republican majorities in the first session of the 119th Congress sought to reverse many of the climate change mitigation programs and investments of then-President Joe Biden’s IRA through the OBBBA, they re-invested the USDA conservation dollars instead of rescinding or repealing them. More evidence of the sustained bipartisan support for these practices came when the Trump Administration launched its own Regenerative Agriculture Pilot Program and sought to advance implementation of the SUSTAINS Act in December 2025.

Takeaway Two: Key Principles Guiding NRCS Conservation Programs Should Continue.

Importantly, roundtable participants also agreed that the way these programs are administered is critical to their ultimate success and continued popularity with farmers and ranchers. As opposed to many types of climate mitigation strategies that may be more regulatory or compulsory in nature, practices deployed across farmland should continue to adhere to key principles, including:

Voluntary: A hallmark of all NRCS conservation programs is that farmers and ranchers participate voluntarily.

Farmer-led and flexible: When it comes to farming and ranching lands, one size does not fit all. Producers need to feel empowered to work with their federal partners at NRCS to craft conservation plans that are appropriate for their land and the region of the country where they are operating. In following the lead of producers, NRCS can ensure that their customers feel that the adoption of regenerative practices on their land is being done with them and not to them.

Outcome-driven: Related to the principle above, agricultural conservation programs should primarily focus on outcomes, rather than some other metric (dollars invested, etc.). Producers know their land better than anyone else and are best equipped to determine—with the help of NRCS technical assistance—where to invest limited federal resources to achieve the biggest impact.

Takeaway Three: Other Policy Levers and Markets Outside of USDA Can Accelerate Practice Adoption.

Roundtable participants observed that emerging opportunities can provide financial incentives for more producers to adopt regenerative practices without relying on traditional cost-share conservation programs at USDA for financing. While farmer uptake of these practices may have been traditionally associated with the portfolio of conservation programs administered by NRCS, other avenues now exist to drive similar investment, such as incentives through the tax code and participation in maturing environmental markets.

Sustainable Transportation Fuels: Demand for transportation fuels with lower carbon intensity (including sustainable aviation fuel, maritime fuels, and more traditional passenger vehicle biofuels) are expected to increase dramatically as economies across the globe seek to decarbonize the transportation sector. Aviation and shipping, in particular, are more difficult to electrify than other forms of transportation. Because global aviation and ocean-based shipping transcend international borders, many flights and ships departing the United States in the coming years will likely rely on a cleaner fuel mix to meet international laws or the regulatory regime in the destination country. Thus, even without a parallel domestic effort, American air carriers and shipping companies may be required to procure a cleaner fuel mix.

Farmers have already produced feedstocks for non-fossil-based fuel additives for decades under the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) and other programs, making them well-positioned to lead in this transition and capture significant global market share. However, the current domestic production quantities for sustainable aviation fuels, cleaner maritime fuels, and low-carbon advanced biofuels are all well below the projected demand. To correct this imbalance, Congress has amended the tax code under Section 45Z to provide a tax credit for these types of fuels and further develop the associated markets.

However, the lack of clarity around implementation of the 45Z Clean Fuel Production Tax Credit has stifled the industry’s growth thus far. USDA released an interim final rule in January 2025 to govern the production of these clean fuel feedstocks and ensure that the emissions-reducing benefits were tangible and verifiable. The Treasury Department issued proposed rules in February 2026 that build on the USDA’s interim final rule and represent a positive step toward giving businesses the certainty and confidence they need to invest at scale. That said, there are remaining steps the administration must take to ensure that agriculture-based feedstocks are eligible for the tax credit provisions. To realize these new markets and accelerate regenerative practices across domestic farmland, participants strongly recommended that the Treasury Department and related federal agencies take the necessary steps to clearly and unambiguously include low-carbon agricultural feedstocks in the final rules and guidelines related to 45Z.

Voluntary Carbon Market (VCM) and Corporate “Insetting:” While the VCM has, to-date, largely prioritized projects outside of agriculture, roundtable participants observed that the market is projected to grow globally as private sector actors continue to adopt and deepen sustainability commitments made to their customers and shareholders. As confidence increases around the durability of emissions reductions and carbon storage associated with regenerative practices, participants remarked that agricultural producers could become beneficiaries in this growing market.

There have been several federal policy efforts in recent years—at USDA, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, and elsewhere—to drive greater certainty and investment in this arena and participants encouraged continued implementation and focus on these authorities and lines of effort. Similarly, observers highlighted a variety of ongoing corporate efforts whereby companies that use agricultural commodities in food and other products pledge to green their own internal supply chains through regenerative practice adoption (sometimes referred to as “insetting”), though the federal policy nexus to these endeavors may be more limited than other opportunities discussed at the convening.

Takeaway Four: Innovation Should be Prioritized, Both for Measurement/Verification and New Technologies.

Roundtable participants uniformly agreed that innovation and new technologies should be embraced to ensure regenerative practice adoption continues and expands. While a host of measuring, monitoring, reporting, and verification (MMRV) tools to validate the emissions benefits of regenerative practices have existed for decades, many of these tools are cumbersome, costly, and may not provide the reliable, precise measurements that today’s market requires. Similarly, a variety of new technologies and regenerative practices have emerged in recent years that can complement the current suite of “tried and true” climate-smart practices already eligible for NRCS cost-share and the focus of much of the historic academic literature.

New Technologies to Reduce Emissions: Roundtable participants emphasized that Congress and the executive branch departments should continue to prioritize consideration of new agricultural technologies, from clearing the regulatory pathway for feed additives that reduce methane from dairy cows to fostering greater adoption of innovative fertilizers that reduce nitrous oxide emissions. Many of these new technologies are novel, and some have observed that the current domestic regulatory pathways for approval are time-consuming and burdensome. These regulations may need to be revisited or amended to ensure that domestic agricultural producers have access to emission-reducing tools that can keep their products competitive in the global marketplace.

Improved MMRV: As popularity and market interest surrounding commodities produced with regenerative practices have grown, so has scrutiny of the legitimacy and durability of the emissions reduction benefits. NRCS has already made some significant strides in this area, and roundtable participants voiced support for additional investment in MMRV from both the public and private sectors to increase confidence in the value of these practices.

Takeaway Five: Challenges Related to Personnel and Efficient Delivery of Conservation-Related Technical and Financial Assistance Remain.

Roundtable participants lamented that personnel shortages and inconsistent staffing levels have plagued NRCS conservation programs for decades, and those challenges persist today. NRCS is the country’s premier field-based agency charged with helping private landowners improve the health and resilience of their land, and yet the staff required to deliver expert technical assistance and administer conservation grants approved by Congress are routinely stretched too thin. Roundtable participants acknowledged that staffing challenges and other factors will likely force NRCS to become more efficient in the future. Commenters opined that—at a time of tremendous interest in these programs, and when the agency is flush with new resources—continued staff losses could jeopardize the potential for significant gains in regenerative practice adoption.

Conclusion

The participants at BPC’s November 2025 roundtable—which spanned the political and stakeholder landscape—uniformly conveyed strong support for increased adoption of regenerative agricultural practices to mitigate climate change and drive new resources into rural communities. Contributors acknowledged and hailed the appreciable achievements in new policy and associated investments realized over the last decade, but participants also indicated that there is still significant potential for growth. To realize this potential, federal policy should adhere to several longstanding principles that have guided this work, and NCRS, aided by Congress, should increase and stabilize agency staffing levels. Additionally, new investments should be made in MMRV and fostering innovative technologies, and policymakers should look to policy levers outside of USDA—the tax code and regulatory approval pathways being prime examples—to unlock new markets and scale up adoption rates for these practices among farmers and ranchers.

title: Airbus vents frustration at GTF engine delays and slow progress on SAF - Runway Girl
description:
Airbus’ frustration with Pratt & Whitney was palpable during its full-year earnings presentation earlier this month, with the European airframer directly blaming its reduced A320 production rate target on GTF engine delivery delays.
Airbus has revised its expected A320 production rate to between 70 and 75 aircraft a month by the end of 2027 — down from a previous monthly target of 75.
Airbus booked 1,000 gross aircraft orders in 2025 and ended the year with a backlog of 8,754 aircraft.
The airframer is now targeting a production rate of 13 A220s a month in 2028, having previously targeted a rate of 14 a month in 2026.
“The progress is slow — too slow — but it’s not zero.
text:
Airbus’ frustration with Pratt & Whitney was palpable during its full-year earnings presentation earlier this month, with the European airframer directly blaming its reduced A320 production rate target on GTF engine delivery delays.

It also expressed frustration about the slow pace at which the sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) market is taking off, but urged observers not to “give up” on what it sees as a “necessary part” of the aviation industry’s decarbonization journey.

Airbus has revised its expected A320 production rate to between 70 and 75 aircraft a month by the end of 2027 — down from a previous monthly target of 75. It expects the rate to stabilize at 75 thereafter.

Speaking at a press conference on 19 February to discuss Airbus’ full-year results — during which it reported a 6% increase in revenue to €73.4 billion ($86.6 billion) and a 33% rise in adjusted earnings before interest and tax to €7.1 billion — chief executive Guillaume Faury did not mince words when it came to who he thinks is responsible for the reduced production rate target.

“Pratt & Whitney’s failure to commit to the number of engines ordered by Airbus is negatively impacting this year’s guidance and the ramp-up trajectory for this year,” said Faury, adding that Airbus will “continue to work hard to enforce our contractual rights.”

The engine manufacturer had to embark on a recall campaign of its PW1000G geared turbofan, which powers A320neo-family aircraft alongside the rival CFM International LEAP-1A, after a powdered metal issue was detected. This has resulted in the grounding of hundreds of aircraft as maintenance work is carried out, as well as delays to new GTF engine deliveries.

“We are very frustrated that [Pratt & Whitney] have decided to reallocate more to the in-service [aircraft]…to the detriment of Airbus, where we think they should do more on increasing capabilities to serve both needs at the same time,” says Faury. “We continue to work with them to make them change the way they manage this.”

Airbus expects to deliver a total of 870 commercial aircraft in 2026, an increase of almost 10% on the 793 aircraft it delivered last year. It anticipates that its adjusted EBIT will rise to €7.5 billion. Airbus booked 1,000 gross aircraft orders in 2025 and ended the year with a backlog of 8,754 aircraft.

The airframer is now targeting a production rate of 13 A220s a month in 2028, having previously targeted a rate of 14 a month in 2026. Monthly production rates for the A330 and A350 remain unchanged at five in 2029 and 12 in 2028, respectively.

Faury’s evident frustration during the earnings conference was not limited to Pratt & Whitney. He also lamented the slow progress on ramping up global sustainable aviation fuel volumes.

“At Airbus, we strongly believe that SAF is a necessary part of the decarbonization roadmap and strategy, but it’s the one that is less in our hands,” he says. “The progress is slow — too slow — but it’s not zero. In Europe, we have a mandate for 2% and then 6% in 2030, and we see the volumes starting to grow in Europe because of the mandate.”

However, he adds: “The tipping point of acceleration of SAF is indeed more difficult to pass than we were expecting.”

Faury believes there should be a “global understanding of what the SAF percentage should look like”, but notes: “Unfortunately, with the fragmentation of the world at the moment, we are not there, and this is slowing down SAF. But we should not give up on SAF.”

LanzaJet CEO Jimmy Samartzis was at the National Ethanol Conference to receive the award; Meg Witty, vice president of corporate and government affairs, represents the company on RFA’s board of directors.

NEC26 interview - Jimmy Samartzis, LanzaJet (3:04)
text:
Pioneering sustainable aviation fuel producer LanzaJet received the Renewable Fuels Association’s 2026 Industry Award at the National Ethanol Conference in Orlando this week. Last year the company became the world’s first to produce jet fuel from ethanol at a commercial-scale facility.

The LanzaJet Freedom Pines Fuels facility in Soperton, Georgia, has an annual production capacity of 10 million gallons of alcohol-to-jet SAF, and it remains the only biorefinery currently producing ethanol-based SAF in the United States. LanzaJet CEO Jimmy Samartzis was at the National Ethanol Conference to receive the award; Meg Witty, vice president of corporate and government affairs, represents the company on RFA’s board of directors.

LanzaJet is supported by a broad range of investors and funders, including Airbus, All Nippon Airways, British Airways, LanzaTech, Microsoft’s Climate Innovation Fund, Mitsui & Co., Shell, Southwest Airlines, the U.S. Department of Energy, and the UK Department for Transport. The company also has been recognized for its impact by TIME, Fortune, MIT, Reuters, S&P Global, and others.

“We’ve made history by, for the first time ever, creating jet fuel that meets the same standards as fossil-based jet fuel with something that’s not oil,” said Samartzis in an interview at NEC. “Ethanol is just an absolutely wonderful molecule that enables us to do that and do that efficiently and to meet the specs. So the opportunity for the United States to stand out as a leader, not only with domestic production, but also as an exporter of ethanol to meet the demands globally is a significant one. And I’d like to believe that we as a company in our technology is a beacon of hope for our farmers and the ethanol producers in our country.”

Learn more about LanzaJet in this interview.

NEC26 interview - Jimmy Samartzis, LanzaJet (3:04)

title: From Biomass to Sustainable Aviation Fuel - Imagine That - Your Saint John
description:
This episode of the Insights Podcast is another in our bioeconomy series, sponsored by the Greenspring Bioinnovation Hub and Bioindustrial Innovation Canada, and features a conversation with Andrew Parsons, the Project Director at Nova Sustainable Fuels, a company owned by UK-based Octopus Energy.
His company has been quietly working in Nova Scotia since 2021 on a project to build a production facility in Guysborough County near Goldsboro that would convert biomass, such as wood chips and sawdust, into sustainable aviation fuel (saf).
It is a big idea that would require 750,000 tons of biomass to produce 165,000 tons of aviation fuel and would be a major boost to the forestry industry, which has struggled for a market for biomass since the closure of Northern Pulp, which consumed 1.3 million tons of biomass annually until its closure.
The project will have its own renewable wind and solar energy that will be able to generate 1.1.GWs of power.
The European market is driving demand for sustainable aviation fuels, and it has legislation in place to ensure that 70% of aviation fuel will be from sustainable sources by 2050.
text:
This episode of the Insights Podcast is another in our bioeconomy series, sponsored by the Greenspring Bioinnovation Hub and Bioindustrial Innovation Canada, and features a conversation with Andrew Parsons, the Project Director at Nova Sustainable Fuels, a company owned by UK-based Octopus Energy. His company has been quietly working in Nova Scotia since 2021 on a project to build a production facility in Guysborough County near Goldsboro that would convert biomass, such as wood chips and sawdust, into sustainable aviation fuel (saf). It is a big idea that would require 750,000 tons of biomass to produce 165,000 tons of aviation fuel and would be a major boost to the forestry industry, which has struggled for a market for biomass since the closure of Northern Pulp, which consumed 1.3 million tons of biomass annually until its closure.

The company has purchased 770 acres of land with tidal access and has already spent tens of millions of dollars developing the case for the project, and has received approval for its initial environmental review. The project will have its own renewable wind and solar energy that will be able to generate 1.1.GWs of power. The project is estimated to cost between $4 and $6 billion and will contribute $1 billion to provincial GDP annually when at full production. The European market is driving demand for sustainable aviation fuels, and it has legislation in place to ensure that 70% of aviation fuel will be from sustainable sources by 2050. This would be the first such plant in North America and one of the first in the world. The final decision is expected in 2028, with the plant completed by 2031.

title: Doubts over European SAF rules threaten cleaner aviation hopes, investors warn

text:
Doubts over whether governments will maintain ambitious targets on boosting the use of sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) are a threat to the industry’s growth and play into the hands of fossil fuel companies, investors warned this week.

Several executives from airlines and oil firms have forecast recently that SAF requirements in the European Union, United Kingdom and elsewhere will be eased or scrapped altogether, potentially upending the aviation industry’s main policy to shrink air travel’s growing carbon footprint.

Such speculation poses a “fundamental threat” to the SAF industry, which mainly produces an alternative to traditional kerosene jet fuel using organic feedstocks such as used cooking oil (UCO), Thomas Engelmann, head of energy transition at German investment manager KGAL, told the Sustainable Aviation Fuel Investor conference in London.

He said fossil fuel firms would be the only winners from questions about compulsory SAF blending requirements.

What is Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF)?

The EU and the UK introduced the world’s first SAF mandates in January 2025, requiring fuel suppliers to blend at least 2% SAF with fossil fuel kerosene. The blending requirement will gradually increase to reach 32% in the EU and 22% in the UK by 2040.

Another case of diluted green rules?

Speaking at the World Economic Forum in Davos in January, CEO of French oil and gas company TotalEnergies Patrick Pouyanné said he would bet “that what happened to the car regulation will happen to the SAF regulation in Europe”.

The EU watered down green rules for car-makers in March 2025 after lobbying from car companies, Germany and Italy.

“You will see. Today all the airline companies are fighting [against the EU’s 2030 SAF target of 6%],” Pouyanne said, even though it’s “easy to reach to be honest”.

While most European airline lobbies publicly support the mandates, Ryanair Group CEO Michael O’Leary said last year that the SAF is “nonsense” and is “gradually dying a death, which is what it deserves to do”.

EU and UK stand by SAF targets

But the EU and the British government have disputed that. EU transport commissioner Apostolos Tzitzikostas said in November that the EU’s targets are “stable”, warning that “investment decisions and construction must start by 2027, or we will miss the 2030 targets”.

UK aviation minister Keir Mather told this week’s investor event that meeting the country’s SAF blending requirement of 10% by 2030 was “ambitious but, with the right investment, the right innovation and the right outlook, it is absolutely within our reach”.

“We need to go further and we need to go faster,” Mather said.

SAF investors and developers said such certainty on SAF mandates from policymakers was key to drawing the necessary investment to ramp up production of the greener fuel, which needs to scale up in order to bring down high production costs. Currently, SAF is between two and seven times more expensive than traditional jet fuel.

Urbano Perez, global clean molecules lead at Spanish bank Santander, said banks will not invest if there is a perceived regulatory risk.

David Scott, chair of Australian SAF producer Jet Zero Australia, said developing SAF was already challenging due to the risks of “pretty new” technology requiring high capital expenditure.

“That’s a scary model with a volatile political environment, so mandate questioning creates this problem on steroids”, Scott said.

Others played down the risk. Glenn Morgan, partner at investment and advisory firm SkiesFifty, said “policy is always a risk”, adding that traditional oil-based jet fuel could also lose subsidies.

Asian countries join SAF mandate adopters

In Asia, Singapore, South Korea, Thailand and Japan have recently adopted SAF mandates, and Matti Lievonen, CEO of Asia-based SAF producer EcoCeres, predicted that China, Indonesia and Hong Kong would follow suit.

David Fisken, investment director at the Australian Trade and Investment Commission, said the Australian government, which does not have a mandate, was watching to see how the EU and UK’s requirements played out.

The US does not have a SAF mandate and under President Donald Trump the government has slashed tax credits available for SAF producers from $1.75 a gallon to $1.

Is the world’s big idea for greener air travel a flight of fancy?

SAF and energy security

SAF’s potential role in boosting energy security was a major theme of this week’s discussions as geopolitical tensions push the issue to the fore.

Marcella Franchi, chief commercial officer for SAF at France’s Haffner Energy, said the Canadian government, which has “very unsettling neighbours at the moment”, was looking to produce SAF to protect its energy security, especially as it has ample supplies of biomass to use as potential feedstock.

Similarly, German weapons manufacturer Rheinmetall said last year it was working on plans that would enable European armed forces to produce their own synthetic, carbon-neutral fuel “locally and independently of global fossil fuel supply chain”.

Scott said Australia needs SAF to improve its fuel security, as it imports almost 99% of its liquid fuels.

He added that support for Australian SAF production is bipartisan, in part because it appeals to those more concerned about energy security than tackling climate change.
